title: The Working Capital Conversation Most Australian SMEs Have Never Had With Their Suppliers
linkedin:
Two sides to your cash cycle. Most businesses only manage one.

Every June, owner-led businesses across Queensland and NSW do exactly the right thing: they focus on the debtors ledger, chase overdue invoices, and try to collect as much as possible before 30 June. That effort is valuable and it moves real cash.

But working capital has two sides. The other side is what you owe your suppliers, and specifically how long you have before that cash needs to leave your account.

For most SMEs, supplier payment terms have never been deliberately managed. The terms are on the invoice. They get paid. Nobody questions whether different terms were available.

Here is what the maths shows. A business purchasing $2.5 million from suppliers annually on net-14 terms keeps about $96,000 in outstanding payables at any point in time. Shift to net-30 and that figure becomes $205,000. The difference, around $109,000, sits in your account rather than your supplier's — without any new revenue and without any debt.

Most suppliers will agree to extended terms for a long-standing, reliable customer. The businesses that benefit from better terms are the ones that had the conversation. Most SMEs never start it.

The end of the financial year is the most natural moment. Suppliers are reviewing arrangements for the new year. A request framed as an FY27 working capital review lands in context, not as a surprise.

On the ProfitPulse blog today: which suppliers to approach first, how to frame the conversation without creating friction, and what the aggregate cash release looks like across a realistic supplier base.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Most cash flow conversations focus on getting paid faster. But there's another question worth asking before the financial year closes tomorrow: what payment terms are you actually on with your suppliers?

For many businesses, those terms have never been negotiated. They're just what was on the first invoice, and they've stayed that way ever since. Shifting even a handful of key suppliers from net-14 to net-30 can release a meaningful chunk of working capital from the very start of the new year.

When did you last review your terms with your top five suppliers?

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness
google:
For owner-operated businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, and New South Wales, the end of the financial year typically centres on one side of the cash flow picture: collecting from customers. Chasing outstanding invoices, reducing the debtors balance before 30 June, and watching what arrives in the account. This work matters and it moves real money.

There is a second side that receives far less attention: what you owe your suppliers, and whether the payment terms on those invoices have ever been deliberately reviewed.

Most businesses accept the terms on a supplier's invoice as fixed. Net 14 days, net 30 days, whatever was agreed at the start. Those terms are rarely revisited, even as the relationship matures and the payment track record builds over years.

The start of a new financial year is the most natural moment for this conversation. Suppliers are reviewing their own commercial arrangements. A request to move to net-30 from July frames itself as a routine working capital review rather than an urgent ask, and most long-standing suppliers respond positively.

The cash improvement from modest term extensions across a handful of key suppliers can be meaningful in the opening months of FY27, with no change to revenue or costs required.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
